title: LLM Model File Creation
spl:
| tstats `security_content_summariesonly` count
        min(_time) as firstTime
        max(_time) as lastTime
    from datamodel=Endpoint.Filesystem
    where Filesystem.file_name IN (
        "*.gguf*",
        "*ggml*",
        "*Modelfile*",
        "*safetensors*"
    )
    by Filesystem.action Filesystem.dest Filesystem.file_access_time Filesystem.file_create_time
       Filesystem.file_hash Filesystem.file_modify_time Filesystem.file_name Filesystem.file_path
       Filesystem.file_acl Filesystem.file_size Filesystem.process_guid Filesystem.process_id
       Filesystem.user Filesystem.vendor_product
    | `drop_dm_object_name(Filesystem)`
    | `security_content_ctime(firstTime)`
    | `security_content_ctime(lastTime)`
    | `llm_model_file_creation_filter`
description: This detection identifies LLM Model File Creation. It supports security monitoring and incident response. Part of Splunk Security Essentials (ESCU) where applicable.
value: This detection identifies LLM Model File Creation. It supports security monitoring and incident response. Part of Splunk Security Essentials (ESCU) where applicable.
grandmaExplanation: This detection identifies LLM Model File Creation — so we know when protection is off, out of date, or bypassed.
implementation: ESCU Hunting detection — not designed for automated alerting. Run ad-hoc or on a low-frequency schedule from Splunk Enterprise Security for analyst-driven investigation. Requires Sysmon EventID 11 ingested and CIM-normalized.
detailedImplementation:
Enterprise Security Detection Rule

"LLM Model File Creation" is a Hunting detection designed for analyst-driven threat hunting rather than automated alerting, sourced from the Splunk Enterprise Security Content Update (ESCU). Hunting detections are not intended for automated alerting. They are run on-demand or on a low-frequency schedule by security analysts investigating specific threat hypotheses.

Prerequisites

• Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x or later with the ES Content Update (ESCU) app installed and up to date.
• Data sources: Sysmon EventID 11. Must be ingested into Splunk and normalized to the Common Information Model (CIM) via the appropriate Technology Add-on.
• MITRE ATT&CK: T1543. Review the ATT&CK matrix for adjacent techniques to identify detection coverage gaps in your environment.

Deployment

1. In Enterprise Security, navigate to Configure → Content → Content Management.
2. Search for "LLM Model File Creation" or filter by Analytic Story.
3. Hunting detections are typically left disabled for automated scheduling. Instead, run them on-demand from the Search bar or schedule at low frequency (daily or weekly).
4. Review results manually — hunting detections cast a wider net and expect analyst judgment to separate signal from noise.
5. When results warrant further investigation, create a Notable Event manually or initiate your incident response workflow.

Tuning and False Positive Management

Known false positives for this detection: None explicitly documented by the upstream ESCU detection — review alerts in business context before suppressing, and consult the linked references for vendor guidance.

• Hunting detections are expected to produce broader result sets that require analyst interpretation. Focus on refining the search scope (time range, specific hosts or users) rather than eliminating all noise.
• Maintain a hunting journal documenting hypotheses tested, results found, and detection improvements identified.
• Share high-fidelity findings with the detection engineering team to convert recurring hunting patterns into automated TTP or Anomaly detections.

Analyst Response Workflow

When reviewing Hunting results:

1. Run the search manually or review scheduled results. Evaluate each returned event against your threat hypothesis — not every result indicates compromise.
2. Cross-reference findings with current threat intelligence, recent security advisories, and outputs from other detection sources.
3. If suspicious activity is confirmed, create a Notable Event or escalate directly to your incident response process with the supporting evidence.
4. Document the hunting exercise: hypothesis, data sources queried, time range, findings, and any detection engineering improvements identified.

Validation

Confirm the required data sources are flowing:

```spl
| tstats count where index=* by index, sourcetype | sort -count
```

Verify the detection is enabled and firing:

```spl
index=_audit action="alert_fired" ss_name="*"
| stats count by ss_name, trigger_time | sort -trigger_time
```
knownFalsePositives: None explicitly documented by the upstream ESCU detection — review alerts in business context before suppressing, and consult the linked references for vendor guidance.
